title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: Notable Decrease in CA LCFS Type 1 Volumes Alongside Price Rise
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights:

(28th April â€“ 04th May)
  • A total of 220,779 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a fall of 60.03% week-over-week (WoW), while all non-zero trades witnessed a fall of 9.42% WoW as 941,840 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price stood at $58.07/MT for Type 1 transfers, recording a 0.43% rise WoW; All Non-Zero transfers rose by 1.64% in price, reaching $59.49/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price rose by 0.49% WoW; on 06th May, the spot price stood at $58.00/MT, and the benchmark price stood at $63.00/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices witnessed a rise of 0.41% WoW. D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices increased by 7.00% WoW each.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

Contrary to last week, the Type 1 trading volumes have decreased this week. The U.S. House of Representatives voted to overturn the EPAâ€™s 2023 approval of Californiaâ€™s zero-emission truck mandates and rescinded a separate waiver for the stateâ€™s low-NOx regulations. In response, the California Air Resources Board stated the move violates the Congressional Review Act and nonpartisan legal guidance. Also, It is still pending approval in the State Assembly Committee on Appropriations.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_596482" align="alignnone" width="998"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 0.43% WoW rise, increasing from $57.82 to $58.07 this week, while all non-zero credit prices increased by 1.64% WoW, from $58.53 to $59.49. Type 1 trades saw 220,779 credits exchanged, reflecting a fall of 60.03% WoW. The total transaction volume for all non-zero trades saw a fall of 9.42%, reaching 941,840 credits.

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_596483" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices increased by 0.49% WoW, reaching $57.93/MT. On 06th May, the average spot price was $58.00/MT, while the benchmark, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $63.00/MT.

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_596484" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 47 trades were reported in the market, with 10 high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $74.00 for oneType 2 transactions at a volume of 10,000 credits. Last week, the lowest transfer by price was $53.16 per credit of Type 2, reported for a transaction at a volume of 75,000 credits. The smallest volume of transfer was for 15 credits of the Type 1 category, undertaken for a price of $57.00. The largest volume transfers of 100,000 credits of the Type 2 category was undertaken for a price of $58.00.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_596485" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] Average (Type 1) spot price reached $58.26 in the month of April, lower than the Average (Type 1) spot prices for March, which stood at $58.44. With last weekâ€™s trading, volumes rose to 9.12 MMT this month, cementing the monthâ€™s volumes to be the highest in the Programâ€™s history.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_596486" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 28th April â€“ 04th May, total VPT recorded a 4.78% rise WoW, with a rise in all non-zero prices.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN weekly price trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices witnessed a rise of 0.41% WoW. D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices increased by 7.00% WoW each. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming dates

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this week

  • Minnesota Bill Directs Study on SAF Production from Wastewater COâ‚‚ and Green Hydrogen. The Minnesota Legislature has introduced a bill directing the Pollution Control Agency to study the feasibility and cost of producing sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) using carbon dioxide captured from wastewater sludge incineration and green hydrogen. The initiative includes designing carbon capture infrastructure, conducting a front-end engineering study for SAF production in the metropolitan area, and reporting by June 30, 2029, on projected costs, job creation, and SAF output potential. A one-time appropriation of $300,000 is allocated to support this effort.
  • C. to End Zero-Emission Vehicle Rebate Program on May 15, 2025. British Columbia will phase out its Go Electric Passenger Vehicle Rebate Program on May 15, 2025, following over a decade of ZEV incentives and the upcoming end of federal EV rebatThe funding for B.C.â€™s Go Electric Passenger Vehicle Rebate Program runs through May 15, 2025, following which the Province will be pausing the program to consider next steps in view of progress to date and the end of the federal governmentâ€™s electric-vehicle rebates in January 2025. People can still purchase or lease an electric vehicle by May 15 and receive a rebate through the Go Electric program. The province, home to nearly 195,000 ZEVs and one of Canadaâ€™s largest charging networks, is now reviewing its clean transportation programs amid economic pressures and progress in EV adoption.
  • Environmental Quality Commission Approves Delay to Clean Fuels Program Deadlines. The Oregon Environmental Quality Commission has approved a one-month extension for compliance with the Clean Fuels Program (CFP), moving the deadline from April 30 to May 30, 2025, due to a cyberattack that disrupted the Oregon Fuels Reporting System. This delay allows regulated parties and the Oregon Department of Environmental Quality (DEQ) additional time to correct data, recalculate credit balances, and acquire necessary credits for compliance. The same extension applies to the Greenhouse Gas Reporting Program's annual report for liquid fuel suppliers, which shares the same reporting platform and data. This alignment is essential because the reporting program supports the Climate Protection Program by determining compliance obligations and the distribution of compliance instruments. Consequently, DEQ has shifted the distribution date for 2025 compliance instruments from June 30 to no later than July 30, 2025, without altering the calculation methodologyâ€”only the distribution timeline has changed.
  • Washington State Passed Second Substitute House Bill 1409, Amending the Clean Fuels Program. Washingtonâ€™s legislature has passed Second Substitute House Bill 1409, amending the Clean Fuels Program to mandate a 45% reduction in the carbon intensity of transportation fuels below 2017 levels by 2038, with an option to increase the target to 55% under certain conditions. The bill adjusts annual reduction schedules, establishes penalties for noncompliance, expands reporting and transparency requirements, and mandates closer monitoring of credit markets. It also strengthens harmonization with other jurisdictions' clean fuel standards and supports the use of in-state biofuel production. Governor approval is pending.
  • Toyota Announces Plans to Introduce Hydrogen-Fueled Electric Trucks. At the 2025 ACT Expo, Toyota Motor North America announced plans to introduce hydrogen-powered Class 8 fuel cell trucks to reduce diesel use in its California logistics fleet, supported by a new on-site hydrogen fueling station and partnerships with Air Liquide and Iwatani. Toyota also unveiled its next-generation Gen 3 fuel cell system, offering 20% more power and efficiency, with a projected lifespan of over 600,000 miles, and shared broader hydrogen ecosystem investments in infrastructure, electrolysis, and biogenic hydrogen production.
  • Washington State Approves First Tax on ZEV Credit Sales, Primarily Impacting Tesla. Washington State has passed HB 2077, a bill that imposes a tax on the sale and holding of zero-emission vehicle (ZEV) credits, directly affecting Tesla, which generates significant credit surpluses. The bill introduces a 2% tax on ZEV credit sales and a 10% tax on banked credits based on market value. Lawmakers argue the measure is intended to prevent excessive profit from the credit system and to level the playing field for automakers transitioning to electric vehicles. The bill awaits Governor Bob Fergusonâ€™s decision, with broader implications for other states following Californiaâ€™s ZEV standards.
  • Pacific Clean Fuels Launches, Expands Access to Clean Hydrogen Fuel Across Central California. Pacific Clean Fuels officially launches with a mission to accelerate California's clean energy transition through expanded hydrogen fuel availability across the Central Valley and beyond. Leveraging innovative technologies like OneH2's high-capacity trailers and mobile fuelers, Pacific Clean Fuels offers a revolutionary approach to hydrogen fuel delivery-simplifying access, reducing upfront infrastructure costs, and supporting scalable decarbonization for businesses in industries ranging from transportation and agriculture to logistics and municipal services.
  • BC Hydro Accelerates EV Charging Network Expansion, Releases New Progress Report. BC Hydro has released its latest report, Driving Forward: BC Hydro's EV Charging Progress Report, detailing significant advancements in its electric vehicle (EV) fast-charging network.â€‹ Over the past year, BC Hydro has tripled its public charging network, adding 418 new charging ports, bringing the total to 591 across British Columbia. This expansion includes 22 new hubs with 8 to 22 charging ports each, and the introduction of 350-kilowatt chargers capable of delivering up to 100 kilometers of range in just five minutes. The network's power output has surged from 10 megawatts in April 2024 to 41 megawatts by March 2025. Approximately 85% of the charging ports are fast chargers supporting CCS, NACS, and CHAdeMO connectors. â€‹Looking ahead, BC Hydro aims to expand its charging network to 800 ports by spring 2026 and will debut its first 400-kilowatt chargers this year, offering 100 kilometers of range in just three minutes. â€‹
